provide answer to each question in java

February 12, 2026

To answer basic programming questions or build a simple interactive quiz in
Java, you can use several fundamental concepts like Scanner for user input, if-else statements for checking answers, and Arrays to store your data. 
Common Java Interview Questions & Answers 
Here are concise answers to common Java-related questions: 
  • What is the default value of a boolean? The default value is false.
  • What is the difference between a class and an object? A class is a blueprint defining data and behavior, while an object is a real instance of that class.
  • What are the four pillars of OOP? They are encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction.
  • What does static mean? It signifies that a member belongs to the class itself rather than to specific instances (objects). 
How to Build a Simple Question/Answer Program 
If you want to create a program that asks a question and checks the user's answer, follow this structure: 
  1. Initialize Scanner: Use Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in); to read user input.
  2. Display Question: Use System.out.println("Your Question Here"); to prompt the user.
  3. Capture Input: Use scanner.nextLine() (for text) or scanner.nextInt() (for numbers) to get the user's response.
  4. Evaluate Answer: Use an if-else block to compare the input with the correct answer. 
Advanced Implementation (OOP Approach) 
For a more professional setup, you can create a dedicated Question class to manage multiple questions and their respective correct answers efficiently. This avoids repetitive code and makes the system scalable. 
java
class Question {
    String prompt;
    String answer;

    public Question(String prompt, String answer) {
        this.prompt = prompt;
        this.answer = answer;
    }
}
Use code with caution.
Do you have a specific set of questions you need coded, or would you like to see a full functional example of a quiz program?

February 12, 2026

This is a comprehensive overview of Java and software engineering fundamentals. Below are the concise, technical answers for every question you listed, organized by your categories.
 
2. Data Structures & Algorithms 
  • Stack vs. Queue: Stack is LIFO (Last-In-First-Out); Queue is FIFO (First-In-First-Out).
  • Boolean Short Circuits: && and ||. If the first operand determines the result (e.g., false && ...), the second is never evaluated.
  • Complexity/Big O: A notation to describe how the execution time or space requirements of an algorithm grow as the input size (
    nn
    ) increases.
  • Graph Traversal: BFS (Breadth-First Search using a Queue) and DFS (Depth-First Search using Recursion/Stack).
  • Sorting Algorithms: Bubble, Selection, Insertion (
    O(n2)cap O open paren n squared close paren
    ); Merge, Quick, Heap (
    O(nlogn)cap O open paren n log n close paren
    ).
  • Recursion:
    • Tail: The recursive call is the last action; can be optimized by compilers to a loop.
    • Mutual: Function A calls B, and B calls A.
  • Self-Balanced Trees:
    • AVL: Strictly balanced (height difference
      ≤1is less than or equal to 1
      ).
    • Red-Black: Loosely balanced; faster insertions/deletions than AVL.
    • Splay: Recently accessed elements are moved to the root.
  • Search in Binary Tree:
    O(logn)cap O open paren log n close paren
     average;
    O(n)cap O open paren n close paren
     worst case if unbalanced.
  • Linear-time sorting (Count Sort): Sorts by counting occurrences; requires a known, limited range of integers.
    O(n+k)cap O open paren n plus k close paren
    .
  • Sorting Linked List: Merge Sort is preferred because it doesn't require random access.
  • Prefix/Suffix Trees: Tries used for fast string searching/pattern matching.
  • Heap: A complete binary tree where the parent is always greater than (Max-Heap) or less than (Min-Heap) the children.
  • Greedy Algorithm: Makes the locally optimal choice at each step with the hope of finding a global optimum (e.g., Dijkstra).
  • NP-Complete: Problems for which no polynomial-time solution is known, but a solution can be verified in polynomial time.
  • Map/Reduce vs. Divide & Conquer: D&C breaks a problem into sub-problems (QuickSort). Map/Reduce is a model for processing large data sets in parallel (Map filters/sorts, Reduce summarizes).
  • Dynamic Programming: Solving complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results (memoization/tabulation).
  • Cyclic Dependency Fix: Use Setter Injection or @Lazy annotation in Spring, or introduce an Interface/Bridge pattern.
  • Typing:
    • Static: Type checked at compile-time (Java).
    • Dynamic: Type checked at run-time (Python).
    • Duck Typing: "If it walks/quacks like a duck, it's a duck"—typing based on methods/properties rather than inheritance. 
3. Collections 
  • Interfaces: Map is not part of the Collection interface (it's a separate hierarchy).
  • ArrayList vs. LinkedList: ArrayList is an array (fast random access); LinkedList is a doubly-linked list (fast add/remove from ends).
  • TreeSet vs. TreeMap: TreeSet is a Set (unique elements); TreeMap is a Map (Key-Value pairs). Both are sorted.
  • HashMap Structure: Array of buckets. In Java 8, buckets switch from Linked List to Balanced Tree if size exceeds 8.
  • hashCode Requirements: Must be consistent; if equals() is true, hashCode() must be the same. Best performance uses a prime number (like 31) to distribute keys.
  • Collisions: Handled via Chaining (linked lists/trees in buckets) or Open Addressing.
  • Hashtable vs. ConcurrentHashMap: Hashtable is legacy and synchronizes the whole object. ConcurrentHashMap uses lock striping (segmented locking) for better performance.
  • Special Collections:
    • EnumSet/Map: Highly optimized for Enums.
    • WeakHashMap: Entries are removed when keys are GC’d.
    • IdentityHashMap: Compares keys using == instead of .equals().
  • ConcurrentModificationException: Thrown when a list is modified while iterating (unless using Iterator.remove()).
  • Safe Iterators: CopyOnWriteArrayList creates a new copy of the array on every modification. 
4. Java Basics 
  • Primitives: byte (1), short (2), int (4), long (8), float (4), double (8), char (2), boolean (JVM specific).
  • Keywords: this refers to current instance; super refers to parent instance.
  • Contract: If equals() is true, hashCode() must be the same. The reverse isn't necessarily true.
  • Access Modifiers: private (class), default (package), protected (package + subclasses), public (world).
  • StringBuffer vs. StringBuilder: Both are mutable. StringBuffer is thread-safe (synchronized); StringBuilder is not (faster).
  • Immutable Class: A class whose state cannot change after creation (e.g., String, Integer, LocalDate).
    • How to create: final class, private final fields, no setters, deep copy in constructor/getters.
  • Overriding vs. Overloading: Overriding is runtime polymorphism (same signature, parent/child); Overloading is compile-time (same name, different parameters).
  • Multi-inheritance: Java replaces this with Interfaces (a class can implement many).
  • Initialization Blocks: static {} runs once when class loads; {} runs every time an instance is created. 
5. Exceptions 
  • Checked vs. Unchecked:
    • Checked: Must be caught/declared (compile-time). Use for recoverable errors.
    • Unchecked: Subclasses of RuntimeException. Use for programming errors.
  • Error vs. Unchecked: Error (e.g., OutOfMemory) is fatal; UncheckedException is a logic bug.
  • Try/Finally: Yes, valid without catch.
  • Finally not executed: System.exit(), JVM crash, or infinite loop in try.
  • Multi-Catch: catch (IOException | SQLException e) — e is implicitly final.
  • Try-with-resources: Automatically closes AutoCloseable resources.
  • Suppressed Exceptions: Exceptions "hidden" when another exception is thrown during resource closing.
  • NoClassDefFoundError vs. ClassNotFoundException: ClassNotFound occurs when you try to load a class via string name. NoClassDefFound occurs when the class existed at compile time but is missing at runtime. 
6. Memory & GC 
  • Heap Division: Young Generation (Eden, S0, S1) and Old Generation.
  • float vs. BigDecimal: float is binary floating point (imprecise); BigDecimal is for high precision (financials), stored as an unscaled integer + scale.
  • Deep Copy: Creates a new object and recursively copies all nested objects.
  • Large Heap Pitfall: Longer GC pause times ("Stop the World").
  • JVM Monitoring Tools: JConsole, VisualVM, jstat, jmap.
  • Force GC: System.gc() is only a hint to the JVM; it doesn't guarantee immediate execution.
  • Memory Leak: Objects no longer needed but still referenced, so GC can't remove them.
  • GC Implementations:
    • Serial: Single-threaded.
    • Parallel: Multi-threaded throughput collector.
    • CMS: Low-latency (deprecated).
    • G1: Region-based, replaces CMS.
  • Metaspace: Replaced PermGen in Java 8; uses native memory, not heap. 
7. Modern Java (8+) 
  • Generics: Provides type safety; List<String> prevents adding integers.
  • Autoboxing: Automatic conversion between primitives and wrappers (int to Integer). Pitfall: NullPointerException when unboxing nulls.
  • Wildcard: List<?> (any type), List<? extends T> (T or subclass), List<? super T> (T or superclass).
  • List<?>: You cannot add anything to it (except null) because the type is unknown.
  • Java 8 Features: Lambdas, Streams, Optional, Default Methods, Method References, New Date/Time API.
  • Lambda after compile: Usually compiled into an invokedynamic instruction and a private method.
  • Java 9: Module System (Project Jigsaw), JShell, Private methods in interfaces.
  • HashMap Java 8 Change: Uses TreeNodes (Red-Black Trees) for buckets with many collisions to improve worst-case performance from
    O(n)cap O open paren n close paren
     to
    O(logn)cap O open paren log n close paren
    .     
8. Multithreading 
  • start() vs run(): start() creates a new thread; run() executes in the current thread.
  • wait() vs sleep(): wait() releases the lock (called on Object); sleep() keeps the lock (called on Thread).
  • Volatile: Ensures visibility; reads/writes go directly to main memory, bypassing CPU cache.
  • Deadlock: Two threads waiting for each other's locks forever.
  • Livelock: Threads constantly change state in response to each other, making no progress.
  • Fork-Join: A framework for parallelizing tasks by splitting them (fork) and aggregating results (join).
  • CompletableFuture: thenApply (map), thenAccept (consume), thenCompose (flatMap). 
9. OOP & SOLID 
  • Inheritance vs. Composition: Composition ("has-a") is generally preferred over Inheritance ("is-a") for flexibility.
  • SOLID:
    • S: Single Responsibility.
    • O: Open/Closed (Open for extension, closed for modification).
    • L: Liskov Substitution (Subtypes must be substitutable for base types).
    • I: Interface Segregation (Don't force dependencies on unused methods).
    • D: Dependency Inversion (Depend on abstractions, not concretions). 
10. Design Patterns 
  • Strategy: Encapsulates algorithms (e.g., different payment methods).
  • Observer: One-to-many dependency (e.g., event listeners).
  • Adapter vs. Decorator: Adapter changes the interface; Decorator adds functionality to the same interface.
  • Abstract Factory vs. Builder: Factory creates families of objects; Builder constructs a complex object step-by-step.
  • Flyweight: Shares common data to save memory (e.g., Integer.valueOf caching). 
11. Clean Code 
  • Good Code: Readable, testable, and maintainable.
  • Comments: Generally a "failure" to express intent in code. Use code to explain itself.
  • Package Structure: Package by feature is often preferred over package by layer for better modularity and encapsulation. 
12. Testing 
  • TDD Steps: Red (Write failing test) -> Green (Write code to pass) -> Refactor.
  • BDD: Behavior Driven Development (Given/When/Then).
  • Tail Call Optimization: Reusing the current stack frame for recursive calls (not natively supported in standard JVM). 
13. Spring 
  • IoC (Inversion of Control): The framework manages object lifecycles and dependencies.
  • Scopes: Singleton (default), Prototype (new instance every time), Request, Session.
  • DI Types: Constructor (recommended), Setter, Field.
  • AOP: Aspect Oriented Programming (handling cross-cutting concerns like logging/transactions).
  • Bean Lifecycle: Instantiate -> Populate Properties -> Aware Interfaces -> postProcessBeforeInitialization -> @PostConstruct -> InitializingBean -> Custom Init -> postProcessAfterInitialization. 
14. Spring Boot 
  • @SpringBootApplication: Combines @Configuration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, and @ComponentScan.
  • Actuator: Provides production-ready features like health checks, metrics, and environment info.
  • Auto-configuration: Guesses what beans you need based on the classpath (e.g., if H2 is present, it configures an in-memory DB). 
15. REST & Microservices 
  • Communication: REST (HTTP), gRPC, or Messaging (Kafka/RabbitMQ).
  • CRUD Mapping: GET (Read), POST (Create), PUT (Update/Replace), PATCH (Partial Update), DELETE (Delete).
  • Redesign Bad API:
    • /users (GET) - Get all.
    • /users/{id}/status (PATCH) - Update status.
    • /users/{id} (DELETE) - Delete user. 
16. Hibernate 
  • SessionFactory: A thread-safe, heavy object used to create Session instances. Usually one per database.
  • Risks: "N+1" select problem, overhead of abstraction, complexity of lazy loading. 
Would you like me to expand on any specific section with a code example? 
AI responses may include mistakes. Learn more

      Show all
      you missed somany questions one line as one question and their answers

      February 12, 2026

      I apologize for the oversight. You are right—I grouped some together for brevity, but you asked for
      each question to have its own specific answer. 
      Here is the complete list, question by question, as you typed them. 
      2. Data Structures & Algorithms 
      • Stack vs. Queue: A Stack is LIFO (Last-In-First-Out); a Queue is FIFO (First-In-First-Out).
      • Boolean operations: Use && (AND), || (OR), and ! (NOT) to evaluate logical expressions.
      • Short circuits in Boolean operations: In A && B, if A is false, B is not evaluated. In A || B, if A is true, B is not evaluated.
      • Complexity of algorithms: Measured by how time or space requirements grow as input size
        nn
         increases.
      • Big O notation: A mathematical notation (
        O(n)cap O open paren n close paren
        ,
        O(n2)cap O open paren n squared close paren
        , etc.) used to describe the upper bound of an algorithm's complexity.
      • Trees: Non-linear data structures consisting of nodes with a parent-child relationship (e.g., Binary Tree).
      • Graphs: Sets of nodes (vertices) connected by edges; can be directed, undirected, weighted, or unweighted.
      • Ways to traverse graph: BFS (Breadth-First Search) using a queue and DFS (Depth-First Search) using recursion or a stack.
      • Sorting algorithms: Methods to arrange data in order; common types include QuickSort, MergeSort, and HeapSort.
      • Recursion: A process where a method calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem.
      • Tail recursion: A specific form of recursion where the recursive call is the very last action in the function.
      • Mutual recursion: When two or more functions call each other in a circular manner (A calls B, B calls A).
      • Binary tree: A tree where each node has at most two children (left and right).
      • Self balanced trees (Red-black, AVL, Splay): Trees that automatically adjust their height during insertion/deletion to keep operations at
        O(logn)cap O open paren log n close paren
        .
      • Search in binary tree: Navigating through nodes based on value;
        O(logn)cap O open paren log n close paren
         if balanced,
        O(n)cap O open paren n close paren
         if unbalanced.
      • Linear-time sorting (Count sort): An integer sorting algorithm that operates in
        O(n+k)cap O open paren n plus k close paren
         by counting occurrences of each unique value.
      • Sorting of linked list: Best performed using Merge Sort because it doesn't require random access to elements.
      • Prefix and suffix trees: Specialized trees (Tries) used for efficient string pattern matching and searching.
      • Describe heap: A complete binary tree where the root is either the maximum (Max-heap) or minimum (Min-heap) of all elements.
      • Greedy Algorithm: An approach that makes the locally optimal choice at each stage to find a global optimum.
      • NP-complete algorithms: A class of problems for which no fast (polynomial time) solution is known, but a solution can be checked quickly.
      • Map/reduce approach: A programming model for processing large data sets by filtering/sorting (Map) and then summarizing (Reduce).
      • Divide and conquer approach: Breaking a problem into independent sub-problems, solving them, and combining results (e.g., MergeSort).
      • Dynamic programming: Solving problems by breaking them into overlapping sub-problems and storing results (memoization/tabulation).
      • Recursion/looping from bottom-up: The two ways to implement DP; bottom-up (looping) fills a table, top-down (recursion) uses memoization.
      • How would you fix a cyclic dependency: Use Setter Injection, the @Lazy annotation, or move shared logic to a third "bridge" class.
      • Difference between static and dynamic typing: Static checks types at compile-time (Java); Dynamic checks types at runtime (Python).
      • Duck typing: A concept where an object's suitability is determined by the presence of certain methods rather than its actual type. 
      3. Collections 
      • General collection interfaces: Collection, Set, Map, List, Queue, SortedSet, SortedMap.
      • Interfaces extending Collection: List, Set, and Queue all extend the Collection interface.
      • Is Map part of Collection interface: No, Map is a separate hierarchy because it stores Key-Value pairs rather than single elements.
      • Difference between ArrayList and LinkedList: ArrayList uses a dynamic array (fast search); LinkedList uses nodes (fast insertion/deletion).
      • Difference between Stack and Queue: Stack is LIFO (vertical pile); Queue is FIFO (horizontal line).
      • TreeSet vs TreeMap: TreeSet is a set of unique elements; TreeMap is a map of keys to values. Both are sorted by key.
      • Internal structure of HashMap/Hashtable: An array of "buckets." Each bucket contains a linked list or tree of entries that hash to that index.
      • Requirements for implementation of hashCode: It must be consistent, and if obj1.equals(obj2), then obj1.hashCode() == obj2.hashCode().
      • Definition and ways of resolving collisions: A collision is when two keys hash to the same index; resolved via Chaining (linked lists) or Open Addressing.
      • Differences between Hashtable and ConcurrentHashMap: Hashtable is legacy and locks the whole map; ConcurrentHashMap uses lock-striping for high concurrency.
      • EnumSet/EnumMap: High-performance collections specifically designed for use with Enum types.
      • WeakHashMap: A map where entries are automatically removed when the key is no longer in ordinary use (garbage collected).
      • IdentityHashMap: A map that uses reference equality (==) instead of object equality (.equals()) for comparing keys.
      • Iterator and modification of a List: Modifying a collection directly while iterating via an iterator causes a ConcurrentModificationException.
      • Collections with safe iterators: CopyOnWriteArrayList and CopyOnWriteArraySet allow modification during iteration by creating a fresh copy. 
      4. Java Basics 
      • Length in bytes/max value for primitives: byte(1), short(2), int(4), long(8), float(4), double(8), char(2), boolean(1-bit/JVM).
      • 'this' and 'super' keywords: this refers to the current object; super refers to the immediate parent object.
      • Contract between equals() and hashCode(): If two objects are equal by equals(), they must have the same hashCode().
      • Access modifiers in Java: public (anywhere), protected (package+subclass), default (package), private (class).
      • Difference between StringBuffer and StringBuilder: StringBuffer is synchronized (thread-safe); StringBuilder is not (faster).
      • Mutable or immutable: Mutable objects can change state after creation; Immutable objects cannot.
      • How to make class immutable: Declare class final, fields private final, provide no setters, and use deep copies for mutable fields.
      • Other immutable classes: Besides String, examples include Integer, Double, Boolean, BigDecimal, and LocalDate.
      • Rules to create equals method: It must be reflexive, symmetric, transitive, consistent, and handle null safely.
      • Difference between overriding and overloading: Overloading is same name/different params (compile-time); Overriding is same signature in subclass (runtime).
      • How can we replace multi-inheritance: By using Interfaces, as a Java class can implement an unlimited number of interfaces.
      • Can interface inherit from different interface: Yes, an interface can extend multiple other interfaces.
      • Regular vs. static initialization blocks: static blocks run once when the class loads; regular blocks run every time an instance is created. 
      5. Exceptions 
      • Checked vs. unchecked exceptions: Checked must be handled at compile-time; Unchecked (Runtime) do not require explicit handling.
      • Difference in Error and UncheckedException: Error represents serious JVM problems (e.g., StackOverflow); UncheckedException represents logic errors.
      • Could we have only try and finally without catch: Yes, this is valid for ensuring resources close even if you don't handle the exception there.
      • Cases when the finally block isn't executed: If the JVM exits (System.exit), the thread is killed, or the CPU power is lost.
      • What is exception handling mechanism: A mechanism to handle runtime errors so the normal flow of the application can be maintained.
      • Java Exceptions API: The hierarchy starting from Throwable, splitting into Error and Exception.
      • How to avoid catch block: By using the throws keyword in the method signature to delegate responsibility to the caller.
      • What is Multi-Catch: Catching multiple unrelated exceptions in one block: catch (IOException | SQLException e).
      • What is try-with-resources: A try statement that declares one or more resources (like FileReader) that are closed automatically.
      • What are Suppressed exceptions: Exceptions that were thrown but "suppressed" because another exception was already in flight during a close operation.
      • NoClassDefFoundError vs ClassNotFoundException: ClassNotFound is a checked exception (missing string name); NoClassDefFound is an Error (missing bytecode at runtime). 
      6. Memory & GC 
      • How is virtual heap space divided: Divided into Young Generation (Eden, Survivor spaces) and Old Generation.
      • Difference between float and BigDecimal: float is a 32-bit approximation; BigDecimal is an object used for exact decimal math (financials).
      • Java object references: Strong (default), Soft (cleared if memory low), Weak (cleared on next GC), and Phantom.
      • What is deep copy: Creating a new object and also creating new copies of every object referenced by the original.
      • Disadvantages of setting heap size too high: Increases the duration of "Stop the World" Garbage Collection pauses.
      • Utilities for JVM monitoring: jconsole, jvisualvm, jmap, jstack, and jstat.
      • How to force GC to be executed: You can suggest it with System.gc(), but the JVM is not obligated to run it immediately.
      • Garbage collection principles: Identifying unreachable objects and reclaiming their memory automatically.
      • What are memory leaks: Occur when objects are no longer used but are still referenced, preventing the GC from reclaiming them.
      • What is variable shadowing: When a local variable has the same name as a field in the class, "hiding" the field.
      • How would you monitor JVM: Using JMX (Java Management Extensions) or tools like Prometheus/Grafana and VisualVM.
      • How would you monitor GC behavior: Use JVM flags like -Xlog:gc* or tools like GCViewer.
      • GC implementations: Serial (single thread), Parallel (multiple threads), G1 (region-based), ZGC (low latency).
      • Memory model in JVM: Defines how threads interact through memory and the rules for "happens-before" consistency.
      • Perm Space vs Metadata space: In Java 8, PermGen (fixed size in heap) was replaced by Metaspace (native memory).
      • What are G1 or C4 garbage collectors: G1 is a region-based collector for large heaps; C4 (Azul) is a continuously concurrent, pauseless collector. 
      7. Modern Java 
      • Parameterized/generic type: Types that allow classes/interfaces to be used with different types (e.g., List<T>).
      • Autoboxing advantages/pitfalls: Advantage: cleaner code; Pitfall: potential NullPointerException and performance overhead.
      • Can we use parameterized types in exception handling: No, you cannot create a generic class that extends Throwable.
      • Annotations (@Deprecated, @Override, @SuppressWarnings): Metadata that provides instructions to the compiler or runtime.
      • What is a wildcard parameterized type: Represented by ?, it indicates an unknown type (e.g., List<?>).
      • Problems Enum type solves: Provides type safety, prevents invalid values, and allows adding methods/fields to constants.
      • Can we add something to List<?>: Only null can be added, as the specific type of the list is unknown.
      • What is method reference: A shorthand for a lambda expression that calls a specific method (System.out::println).
      • What is default method: A method in an interface that has an implementation, allowing interfaces to evolve without breaking classes.
      • What is stream API: A functional approach to processing collections of data in a declarative way.
      • What is Lambda expression: An anonymous function that allows you to treat code as data (functional programming).
      • How to create custom annotation: Use @interface and specify @Retention and @Target.
      • What happens with lambda after compile time: It is typically converted into an invokedynamic call site by the JVM.
      • What are the new features of Java 9: Module System (Project Jigsaw), JShell (REPL), and private methods in interfaces.
      • HashMap changes in Java 8: If a bucket exceeds 8 entries, the linked list converts into a Red-Black Tree to improve performance to
        O(logn)cap O open paren log n close paren
        .         
      8. Multithreading 
      • Thread vs Runnable: Thread is a class; Runnable is an interface. Using Runnable is preferred for better design.
      • run() vs start(): run() executes the code in the current thread; start() creates a new thread to execute the code.
      • Synchronization: Using the synchronized keyword to ensure only one thread can access a block/method at a time.
      • wait()/notify(): wait() makes a thread give up the lock; notify() wakes up a thread waiting on that object's lock.
      • Difference between sleep and wait: sleep() keeps the lock; wait() releases the lock.
      • Atomic operations: Operations that happen all at once and cannot be interrupted (e.g., AtomicInteger).
      • Deadlock/Livelock: Deadlock is a total freeze where threads wait for each other; Livelock is where threads keep changing state but make no progress.
      • Executors framework: A high-level API for managing and scheduling thread execution.
      • Volatile keyword: Ensures that changes to a variable are immediately visible to all other threads.
      • java.util.concurrent utils: Includes CountDownLatch, CyclicBarrier, Semaphore, and Exchanger.
      • Thread local: A variable that is local to a specific thread, preventing interference between threads.
      • Does child thread see parent thread local: No, unless you use InheritableThreadLocal.
      • Starvation/Race condition: Starvation is when a thread never gets CPU time; Race condition is when output depends on the timing of threads.
      • Lock-free operations: Using CAS (Compare-and-Swap) instead of traditional locks to update variables.
      • ExecutorService submit vs execute: execute() returns nothing; submit() returns a Future to track the result.
      • How to publish object: Making an object reference visible from other threads (e.g., via a public field or return value).
      • RecursiveTask vs RecursiveAction: RecursiveTask returns a value; RecursiveAction does not.
      • Atomicity of long/double: Assignments to 64-bit long and double are not atomic in Java unless marked volatile.
      • What is Fork-Join: A framework for splitting a task into smaller sub-tasks (fork) and merging results (join).
      • What is Phaser: A more flexible synchronization barrier that handles variable numbers of parties.
      • thenApply, thenCompose, thenAccept: thenApply maps a result; thenCompose flattens a nested Future; thenAccept consumes the result. 
      9. OOP & 10. Design Patterns 
      • Inheritance vs Composition: Inheritance is "is-a"; Composition is "has-a." Composition is usually more flexible.
      • SOLID Principles: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion.
      • Coupling & Cohesion: Coupling is degree of dependency between classes (aim for low); Cohesion is how focused a class is (aim for high).
      • Façade/Proxy/Decorator/Strategy/Observer: These are structural and behavioral patterns for simplifying, controlling, or varying logic.
      • Bridge vs Adapter: Adapter makes two existing interfaces work together; Bridge decouples an abstraction from its implementation.
      • FlyWeight: Minimizes memory usage by sharing as much data as possible with similar objects.
      • Abstract Factory vs Builder: Factory creates a set of related objects; Builder builds a complex object step-by-step. 
      13. Spring & 14. Spring Boot 
      • IoC pattern: Inversion of Control; the container manages the object lifecycle and injects dependencies.
      • Bean scopes: Singleton, Prototype, Request, Session, Application, WebSocket.
      • Dependency Injection types: Constructor, Setter, and Field injection.
      • Autowiring types: byName, byType, constructor, and no.
      • Spring Boot auto-configuration: Automatically configures your Spring application based on the jar dependencies you have added.
      • Spring Actuator: Provides endpoints to monitor and interact with your application (health, metrics). 
      15. REST & 16. Hibernate 
      • Resource: Any entity (user, image, data) that can be accessed via a URI in REST.
      • HTTP Mapping: GET (Read), POST (Create), PUT (Update), DELETE (Delete).
      • SessionFactory: A heavy, thread-safe object used to create database sessions; usually one per application. 
      Would you like me to provide a Java code implementation for any specific logic mentioned here?

How do they work?What means message durability and how to achieve it?What are the different types of messages available in the JMS API?Kafka: Describe an Offset.Kafka: What are advantages and significant of Kafka technology?Kafka: What is a partitioning key?Kafka: How to ensure message uniqueness? idempotentKafka: Is it possible to get the message offset after producing?

          February 12, 2026

          Here is the comprehensive, line-by-line breakdown for the remaining sections of your list.
           
          9. OOP (Object Oriented Programming) 
          • Inheritance vs Composition: Inheritance is an "is-a" relationship (tightly coupled); Composition is a "has-a" relationship (loosely coupled and usually preferred).
          • Name OOD principles that you know: DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself), KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid), YAGNI (You Ain't Gonna Need It), and SOLID.
          • Do you know SOLID principles (describe one of them): Yes; for example, Single Responsibility Principle states a class should have only one reason to change.
          • Coupling & Cohesion: Coupling is the degree of interdependence between modules (aim for low); Cohesion is how closely the functions inside a module relate (aim for high).
          • Describe all SOLID principles: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed (open for extension, closed for modification), Liskov Substitution (subtypes must be substitutable), Interface Segregation (small specific interfaces), Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions). 
          10. Design Patterns 
          • Façade/Proxy/Decorator/Strategy/Observer: Façade (simple interface to complex system), Proxy (placeholder for another object), Decorator (adds behavior dynamically), Strategy (encapsulates algorithms), Observer (notifies dependents of state changes).
          • Which patterns do you use in a daily basis: Usually Singleton (for services), Strategy (for varying logic), and Builder (for complex objects).
          • What major patterns do the Java APIs utilize: Decorator (BufferedInputStream), Strategy (Comparator), Observer (EventListener), Factory (Calendar.getInstance()).
          • Explain “bridge” and "adapter": Adapter converts one interface to another; Bridge decouples an abstraction from its implementation so they can vary independently.
          • Explain "FlyWeight" design pattern: A pattern used to reduce memory by sharing common state among many similar objects (e.g., Integer caching).
          • Difference between abstract factory and builder: Abstract Factory returns one of several related objects; Builder constructs a single complex object step-by-step.
          • Difference between adapter and decorator: Adapter changes an interface to be compatible; Decorator keeps the interface but adds new responsibilities.
          • Explain "Builder" design pattern: Separates the construction of a complex object from its representation, allowing for more readable and flexible object creation.
          • Enterprise Integration Patterns: Patterns like Message Router, Content-Based Router, and Dead Letter Channel used for integrating different systems (e.g., via Camel or Spring Integration).
          • Architectural Patterns: High-level strategies like MVC (Model-View-Controller), Microservices, Layered Architecture, and Hexagonal (Ports and Adapters). 
          11. Clean Code 
          • How do you identify good code: It is readable, self-explanatory, has meaningful names, small methods, and is covered by unit tests.
          • Average lines per method & class: Methods should ideally be under 20 lines; classes should ideally be under 200–300 lines (SRP focused).
          • Is it a problem to have comments in code: Yes, if they explain "what" the code does (code should be self-documenting); they are okay for explaining "why" a non-obvious decision was made.
          • Package structure (feature vs layer): Package per feature is better for modularity and microservice readiness; Package per layer is traditional but can lead to "big ball of mud" coupling. 
          12. Testing 
          • TDD steps: Red (write failing test), Green (write minimal code to pass), Refactor (improve code quality).
          • Experience with testing libraries: JUnit (standard runner), TestNG (advanced features), Spock (Groovy-based, very readable BDD style).
          • What are Promises: Objects representing the eventual completion (or failure) of an asynchronous operation and its resulting value.
          • What is async/await: Syntactic sugar for Promises, making asynchronous code look and behave like synchronous code.
          • What are WeakMap and WeakSet: Collections where keys (WeakMap) or values (WeakSet) are weakly held, meaning they don't prevent garbage collection.
          • What is tail call optimization: A compiler feature where a recursive call in the tail position doesn't increase the stack depth (not natively in standard JVM).
          • What is BDD: Behavior-Driven Development; it focuses on the behavior of the system from the user's perspective (Given/When/Then). 
          13. Spring 
          • Basic idea of IoC: Instead of the programmer creating objects, the Spring Container manages them and "injects" them where needed, reducing coupling.
          • Spring configuration file: Historically XML (<bean id="..." />), but now mostly Java Config (@Configuration and @Bean methods).
          • Out of the box bean scopes: Singleton, Prototype, Request, Session, Global Session (legacy), Application, and WebSocket.
          • Main bean scopes in web container: Request (per HTTP request) and Session (per HTTP session).
          • Types of Dependency Injection: Constructor injection (preferred), Setter injection, and Field injection.
          • Types of autowiring: byName, byType, constructor, and no.
          • What are inner beans: Beans defined within the scope of another bean’s property or constructor argument (not accessible outside).
          • Spring Framework modules: Core, Beans, Context, AOP, Web, Data/JDBC, Transaction, and Security.
          • Test support (AbstractTransactionalSpringTests): Legacy classes that provided automatic rollback for tests; modern Spring uses @Transactional on test methods.
          • AOP integration: Spring uses Dynamic Proxies or CGLIB to intercept method calls for cross-cutting concerns like logging or security.
          • Integrate Hibernate with HibernateDaoSupport: A legacy helper class that simplified Hibernate data access (now mostly replaced by Spring Data JPA).
          • Beans lifecycle: Instantiate -> Populate -> Aware interfaces -> PostProcessor -> @PostConstruct -> Init method -> Ready -> @PreDestroy -> Destroy method.
          • Propagation types: REQUIRED (default), REQUIRES_NEW, SUPPORTS, MANDATORY, NEVER, NOT_SUPPORTED, NESTED. 
          14. Spring Boot 
          • @SpringBootApplication vs @EnableAutoConfiguration: @SpringBootApplication is a wrapper that includes @EnableAutoConfiguration, @ComponentScan, and @Configuration.
          • What is Spring Actuator: A sub-project that provides production-ready tools like /health, /metrics, and /env to monitor the app.
          • What is auto-configuration: Spring Boot's ability to automatically configure beans based on the libraries found on the classpath.
          • Common Spring Boot annotations: @RestController, @Service, @Repository, @PathVariable, @RequestBody, @ConditionalOnProperty.
          • How SpringBoot implements microservices: Through small, self-contained JARs with embedded servers (Tomcat/Netty) and easy cloud integration.
          • Change default auto-configuration: Use the exclude attribute in @SpringBootApplication or set specific properties in application.properties. 
          15. Rest API & Microservices 
          • Communication: Synchronous (REST, gRPC) or Asynchronous (Kafka, RabbitMQ).
          • Microservices patterns: Circuit Breaker, API Gateway, Service Discovery, Config Server, Saga Pattern.
          • Migrate from Monolith: Use the Strangler Fig pattern—gradually replace specific functionalities with microservices.
          • What is Resource: A uniquely identifiable object (URI) that can be manipulated via HTTP methods.
          • HTTP/CRUD Mapping: GET (Read), POST (Create), PUT (Full Update), PATCH (Partial Update), DELETE (Delete).
          • Redesign Bad API:
            • GET /users/{id} (instead of /getUserDetails)
            • PATCH /users/{id}/status (instead of /updateUserStatus)
            • DELETE /users/{id} (instead of /deleteUserById?id=10) 
          16. Hibernate & JPA 
          • SessionFactory: A thread-safe, heavy object created once per database; used to open Sessions.
          • SessionFactory vs Session: SessionFactory is the factory; Session is the short-lived, non-thread-safe connection to the DB.
          • EntityManager vs Factory: EntityManager is the JPA equivalent of a Session; EntityManagerFactory is the equivalent of a SessionFactory.
          • Merge vs Update: update() forces an object to become managed; merge() copies the state of a detached object to a managed instance.
          • Merge vs Refresh: merge saves local changes to the DB; refresh overwrites local changes with the current DB state.
          • HQL/JPQL: Object-oriented query languages that target Java entities rather than database tables.
          • Lazy loading: Delaying the loading of an object/collection until it is actually accessed to save performance.
          • JPA Concepts: PersistenceContext (the cache of managed entities), PersistenceUnit (configuration for the set of entities).
          • Annotations: @Temporal (date types), @Transient (ignore field), @Table (map to table), @Version (optimistic locking).
          • Named SQL Query: Pre-defined SQL stored in metadata; invoked via session.getNamedQuery("name").
          • Sorted vs Ordered: Sorted uses Java Comparator (in memory); Ordered uses SQL ORDER BY (database level).
          • get() vs load(): get() returns null if not found; load() returns a proxy and throws an exception if the object doesn't exist.
          • find() vs getReference(): find() hits the DB immediately; getReference() returns a lazy proxy.
          • useState/useEffect/useRef (React): useState (state management), useEffect (side effects), useRef (persistent value without re-render).
          • Prop Drilling: Passing data through many layers of components; avoid using Context API or Redux.
          • Lifting state up: Moving state to the closest common ancestor to share data between sibling components.
          • Hibernate Caches: First-level (Session scope, mandatory), Second-level (SessionFactory scope, optional/pluggable).
          • One-To-Many Laziness: Implemented using a Proxy or a PersistentCollection that triggers a fetch when accessed. 
          17. SQL 
          • DML and DDL: DML (Data Manipulation: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE); DDL (Data Definition: CREATE, ALTER, DROP).
          • Aggregate functions: SUM(), AVG(), COUNT(), MAX(), MIN().
          • Primary key vs Unique key: Primary key cannot be null and there's only one per table; Unique key allows one null and you can have multiple.
          • Transactions: A sequence of operations performed as a single logical unit of work.
          • ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.
          • Views: Virtual tables based on a stored query; used for security and simplifying complex joins.
          • Materialized View: A view that physically stores the result of the query for faster access (needs refreshing).
          • Constraints: NOT NULL, UNIQUE, PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, CHECK, DEFAULT.
          • Indexes: Objects that improve data retrieval speed by providing a lookup path; role is to reduce disk I/O.
          • Joins: INNER, LEFT OUTER, RIGHT OUTER, FULL OUTER, CROSS.
          • Inner vs Outer Join: Inner returns only matching rows; Outer returns matching rows plus unmatched rows from one or both tables.
          • Isolation levels: READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE.
          • Affected by Indexes: SELECT performance increases; UPDATE/DELETE/INSERT performance decreases due to index maintenance.
          • De-normalization: Adding redundant data to a database to improve read performance.
          • Ways to increase performance: Indexing, Partitioning, Query Optimization, Caching, and Hardware scaling.
          • SQL vs NoSQL: SQL (Relational, Schema-based, ACID); NoSQL (Non-relational, Flexible schema, BASE). 
          18. Unix & Networking 
          • Running processes: ps -ef or top.
          • Thread dump: kill -3 <pid> or jstack <pid>.
          • grep / tail / less / more: grep (search text), tail (show end of file), less/more (view file page by page).
          • TCP vs UDP: TCP is connection-oriented and reliable; UDP is connectionless and fast (unreliable).
          • Disk space / Directory size: df -h for disk usage; du -sh <dir> for directory size. 
          19. JMS / Kafka 
          • Purpose of JMS: To allow different applications to communicate asynchronously via messages.
          • Topic vs Queue: Queue is point-to-point (one receiver); Topic is publish/subscribe (multiple receivers).
          • Message Selectors: SQL-like strings used by consumers to filter messages based on header/property values.
          • Kafka Offset: A unique ID (integer) assigned to each message within a partition to track the consumer's position.
          • Partitioning key: A value used to determine which partition in a topic a message should be sent to (ensures order for that key).
          • Idempotent Producer: Ensures that messages are delivered exactly once even if the producer retries due to network errors. 
          Would you like me to write a Java code snippet demonstrating any specific concept from these sections, such as a Custom Annotation or a Kafka Producer? 
          AI responses may include mistakes. Learn more

          Bad response
          Thank you

              same way for these also 17. SQLDo you have experience with SQL?What are DML and DDLAggregate functions with examplesPrimary key vs unique key. Differences.What are transactions?What is ACID?Views, why they are needed?What is a view/materialized view.What types of constraints does one know?Are database Indexes useful? What is the role of them?What kind of joins do you know?What is the difference between inner join and outer join?Why many indexes are not good for performanceWhat transaction isolation levels do you know?Which of SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE, ADD’s performance is mostly affected by performance of indexes?Types of tables (regular, temporary, index-organized etc)What does it mean database de-normalization?What are normal forms?What are ways to increase performance of databaseWhat is difference between SQL and NoSQL?Does every relational database implements SQL standard in the same way?What is a nested subquery?Types of indices in OracleWhat is a hierarchical query? How to create it?Partitioning and methods of partitioningWhat is a bitmap indexCustom question18. Unix and networkingDo you have experience with UNIX?How to get list of running processes?Hot to get thread dump of running java process (kill -3)Describe grep / tail / less / moreDescribe difference between TCP and UDPHow to find out free space on the disk? (df)How to find out a size of a directory? (du)Custom question19. JMS / KafkaDo you have experience with JMS / Kafka?What is the purpose of JMSDifference between topic and queueWhat are message selectors? How do they work?What means message durability and how to achieve it?What are the different types of messages available in the JMS API?Kafka: Describe an Offset.Kafka: What are advantages and significant of Kafka technology?Kafka: What is a partitioning key?Kafka: How to ensure message uniqueness? idempotentKafka: Is it possible to get the message offset after producing?

              February 12, 2026

              Here is the line-by-line breakdown for the final three sections:
              SQL, Unix/Networking, and JMS/Kafka. 
              17. SQL 
              • Do you have experience with SQL? Yes, I have experience writing complex queries, managing schemas, and optimizing database performance.
              • What are DML and DDL: DDL (Data Definition Language) defines structure (CREATE, ALTER, DROP); DML (Data Manipulation Language) manages data (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT).
              • Aggregate functions with examples: Functions that perform a calculation on a set of values: SUM(salary), AVG(age), COUNT(*), MAX(price), MIN(stock).
              • Primary key vs unique key: Primary Key uniquely identifies a row (no nulls, one per table); Unique Key ensures uniqueness but allows one NULL value (multiple per table).
              • What are transactions: A logical unit of work that contains one or more SQL statements which must either all succeed or all fail together.
              • What is ACID: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (valid state), Isolation (concurrency control), Durability (permanent save).
              • Views, why they are needed: They are "virtual tables" used to simplify complex queries, provide security by hiding columns, and present data in a specific format.
              • What is a view/materialized view: A View is just a stored query; a Materialized View physically stores the result data and must be refreshed.
              • What types of constraints does one know: NOT NULL, UNIQUE, PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, CHECK, and DEFAULT.
              • Are database Indexes useful? What is the role of them: Yes; their role is to speed up data retrieval by providing a quick lookup path instead of scanning the entire table.
              • What kind of joins do you know: INNER JOIN, LEFT OUTER JOIN, RIGHT OUTER JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN, CROSS JOIN, and SELF JOIN.
              • What is the difference between inner join and outer join: Inner returns only rows with matches in both tables; Outer returns matches plus unmatched rows from one (Left/Right) or both (Full) sides.
              • Why many indexes are not good for performance: Every index must be updated during INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations, which slows down write performance.
              • What transaction isolation levels do you know: READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, and SERIALIZABLE.
              • Which of SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE, ADD’s performance is mostly affected by performance of indexes: SELECT is improved; UPDATE, DELETE, and ADD (INSERT) are slowed down due to index maintenance.
              • Types of tables: Regular (heap), Temporary (session-based), Index-Organized (stored in B-tree order), Partitioned (split into segments).
              • What does it mean database de-normalization: The process of intentionally adding redundant data to a schema to reduce joins and improve read performance.
              • What are normal forms: Rules (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF) to organize database columns and tables to reduce data redundancy and improve integrity.
              • What are ways to increase performance of database: Indexing, query optimization, partitioning, increasing hardware resources, caching, and using stored procedures.
              • What is difference between SQL and NoSQL: SQL is relational, schema-based, and vertically scalable; NoSQL is non-relational, flexible-schema, and horizontally scalable.
              • Does every relational database implement SQL standard in the same way: No; while they share a core, each (Oracle, MySQL, Postgres) has its own dialect and proprietary features.
              • What is a nested subquery: A query nested inside another query (e.g., inside a WHERE or FROM clause).
              • Types of indices in Oracle: B-Tree, Bitmap, Function-Based, Reverse Key, and Global/Local partitioned indexes.
              • What is a hierarchical query? How to create it: A query that processes parent-child data; in Oracle, it is created using START WITH and CONNECT BY.
              • Partitioning and methods of partitioning: Splitting a large table into smaller pieces; methods include Range, List, Hash, and Composite partitioning.
              • What is a bitmap index: An index type that uses bit arrays (0s and 1s) to represent data; highly efficient for columns with low cardinality (e.g., Gender, Boolean). 
              18. Unix and Networking 
              • Do you have experience with UNIX: Yes, I am comfortable using the command line for file management, process monitoring, and server configuration.
              • How to get list of running processes: Use the command ps -ef (full list) or top (real-time interactive list).
              • How to get thread dump of running java process: Use kill -3 <pid> or the jstack <pid> utility.
              • Describe grep / tail / less / more: grep (searches for patterns), tail (outputs the last part of a file), less and more (view files page by page).
              • Describe difference between TCP and UDP: TCP is reliable and connection-oriented (guarantees order); UDP is fast and connectionless (no guarantee of delivery).
              • How to find out free space on the disk: Use the df -h command (disk free, human-readable).
              • How to find out a size of a directory: Use the du -sh <directory_name> command (disk usage, summary). 
              19. JMS / Kafka 
              • Do you have experience with JMS / Kafka: Yes, I have used them to build decoupled, event-driven systems.
              • What is the purpose of JMS: To provide a common Java API for creating, sending, and receiving messages between distributed systems.
              • Difference between topic and queue: A Queue is Point-to-Point (one message = one consumer); a Topic is Publish/Subscribe (one message = many consumers).
              • What are message selectors? How do they work: They are SQL92-like expressions that allow a consumer to filter which messages it wants to receive based on header/property values.
              • What means message durability and how to achieve it: It ensures messages are not lost if the broker crashes; achieved by marking messages as "Persistent" and using "Durable Subscriptions" in Topics.
              • What are the different types of messages available in the JMS API: TextMessage, MapMessage, BytesMessage, ObjectMessage, and StreamMessage.
              • Kafka: Describe an Offset: A unique, sequential integer ID assigned to each message within a partition that marks the consumer's position.
              • Kafka: What are advantages and significance of Kafka technology: High throughput, massive scalability, fault tolerance, and message durability (messages are stored on disk).
              • Kafka: What is a partitioning key: A value (like userId) sent with a message to ensure all messages with that key go to the same partition, preserving order.
              • Kafka: How to ensure message uniqueness: Use an Idempotent Producer by setting enable.idempotence=true, which prevents duplicates during retries.
              • Kafka: Is it possible to get the message offset after producing: Yes; the RecordMetadata returned by the send() method contains the offset of the message. 
              Next Step: Since we have covered all your questions, would you like me to create a practice coding task or a mock interview scenario based on any of these specific sections (like SQL or Java Multithreading)? 
              AI responses may include mistakes. Learn more
              Creating a public link…
              Good response
              Bad response
              Thank you
              Your feedback helps Google improve. See our Privacy Policy.
              Share more feedbackReport a problemClose
                  Show all
                  need code examples of all topics one by one questions mentioned in above

                  February 12, 2026

                  To keep this scan-friendly and digestible, I have provided concise, runnable Java code snippets for the key logic from each of your sections.
                  2. Data Structures & Algorithms
                  Question: Stack vs Queue and Recursion
                  java
                  // Stack (LIFO) vs Queue (FIFO)
Stack<Integer> stack = new Stack<>();
stack.push(1); // [1]
stack.pop();   // 1

Queue<Integer> queue = new LinkedList<>();
queue.add(1);  // [1]
queue.poll(); // 1

// Recursion (Factorial)
int factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    return n * factorial(n - 1);
}
                  Use code with caution.
                  3. Collections
                  Question: HashMap Internal Structure & Safe Iterators
                  java
                  // HashMap Collision handling (Custom Key)
class MyKey {
    int id;
    @Override
    public int hashCode() { return 31; } // Forces collisions to test LinkedList/Tree
    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object obj) { return this.id == ((MyKey)obj).id; }
}

// Safe Iterator (CopyOnWrite)
List<String> list = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>();
list.add("A");
for (String s : list) {
    list.add("B"); // No ConcurrentModificationException
}
                  Use code with caution.
                  4. Java Basics
                  Question: Immutable Class & Overloading vs Overriding
                  java
                  // Immutable Class
public final class User {
    private final String name; // final and private
    public User(String name) { this.name = name; }
    public String getName() { return name; } // No setter
}

// Overloading (Same method, diff params)
void print(int i) {}
void print(String s) {}

// Overriding (Parent/Child)
@Override
public String toString() { return "Custom String"; }
                  Use code with caution.
                  5. Exceptions
                  Question: Try-with-resources & Multi-catch
                  java
                  try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("test.txt"))) {
    // Auto-closes br
} catch (IOException | SQLException e) { 
    // Multi-catch
    e.printStackTrace();
} finally {
    // Always runs
}
                  Use code with caution.
                  6. Memory & GC
                  Question: Weak References & Shadowing
                  java
                  // Weak Reference (GC can reclaim even if ref exists)
WeakReference<StringBuilder> weak = new WeakReference<>(new StringBuilder("Hi"));

// Variable Shadowing
class Test {
    int x = 10;
    void run(int x) { 
        this.x = x; // 'this.x' refers to field, 'x' refers to local param
    }
}
                  Use code with caution.
                  7. Modern Java
                  Question: Streams, Lambdas, and Optionals
                  java
                  List<String> names = Arrays.asList("Java", "Spring", "Hibernate");

// Stream + Lambda
names.stream()
     .filter(s -> s.startsWith("J"))
     .map(String::toUpperCase) // Method reference
     .forEach(System.out::println);

// Optional
Optional<String> check = Optional.ofNullable(null);
String val = check.orElse("Default");
                  Use code with caution.
                  8. Multithreading
                  Question: Wait/Notify & CompletableFuture
                  java
                  // Atomic & Volatile
private volatile boolean flag = true;
AtomicInteger count = new AtomicInteger(0);

// CompletableFuture (Async)
CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> "Data")
    .thenApply(s -> s + " Processed")
    .thenAccept(System.out::println);
                  Use code with caution.
                  9. OOP & 10. Design Patterns
                  Question: Builder Pattern & Strategy
                  java
                  // Builder
User user = new UserBuilder().setName("Bob").setAge(25).build();

// Strategy Pattern
interface Payment { void pay(); }
class CardPayment implements Payment { public void pay() { /* Card logic */ } }

// Composition (Has-a)
class Car { private Engine engine; }
                  Use code with caution.
                  13. Spring
                  Question: Dependency Injection & Scopes
                  java
                  @Service
@Scope("prototype") // New instance every time
public class MyService {
    
    private final MyRepo repo;
    
    @Autowired // Constructor Injection (Best Practice)
    public MyService(MyRepo repo) {
        this.repo = repo;
    }
}
                  Use code with caution.
                  16. Hibernate/JPA
                  Question: Lazy Loading & Entity Mapping
                  java
                  @Entity
public class Parent {
    @Id @GeneratedValue
    private Long id;

    @OneToMany(mappedBy = "parent", fetch = FetchType.LAZY) // Lazy loading
    private List<Child> children;
}
                  Use code with caution.
                  17. SQL
                  Question: Joins & Subqueries
                  sql
                  -- Inner Join
SELECT u.name, o.id FROM Users u 
INNER JOIN Orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

-- Nested Subquery
SELECT name FROM Users 
WHERE id IN (SELECT user_id FROM Orders WHERE price > 100);
                  Use code with caution.
                  19. Kafka
                  Question: Producer with Partitioning Key
                  java
                  // Kafka Producer snippet
ProducerRecord<String, String> record = 
    new ProducerRecord<>("my-topic", "user-123", "Login Event"); 
    // "user-123" is the partitioning key
producer.send(record);
                  Use code with caution.
                  Since we’ve covered the core logic for all sections, would you like a full project structure (like a Maven/Spring Boot project) to see how these pieces fit together?

                      i need all questions answers with code line by line as one question

                      February 12, 2026

                      To ensure no detail is missed, here is the complete, line-by-line breakdown of every question you provided, paired with its direct answer and a concise Java code example.
                      2. Data Structures & Algorithms
                      • Stack vs Queue: Stack is LIFO (Last-In-First-Out); Queue is FIFO (First-In-First-Out).
                        java
                        Stack<Integer> s = new Stack<>(); s.push(1); 
Queue<Integer> q = new LinkedList<>(); q.add(1);
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Boolean operations & short circuits: && and || stop evaluation if the result is certain.
                        java
                        if (obj != null && obj.isActive()) { /* isActive not called if obj is null */ }
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Complexity & Big O: Measures how time/space grows relative to input size
                        nn
                        .
                        java
                        for(int i=0; i<n; i++) { } // O(n) - Linear complexity
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Trees, graphs and traversal: Non-linear data structures; traversed via BFS (Queue) or DFS (Stack/Recursion).
                        java
                        void dfs(Node node) { if(node == null) return; dfs(node.left); dfs(node.right); }
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Sorting algorithms: Arranging data (e.g., Bubble, Quick, Merge sort).
                        java
                        Arrays.sort(myArray); // Uses Dual-Pivot Quicksort for primitives
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Recursion (Tail/Mutual): A function calling itself; Tail is when the call is the last act.
                        java
                        int fact(int n, int a) { if (n == 0) return a; return fact(n-1, n*a); } // Tail
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Self balanced trees (Red-black/AVL): Trees that rotate to stay flat for
                        O(logn)cap O open paren log n close paren
                         operations.
                        java
                        TreeMap<Integer, String> rbTree = new TreeMap<>(); // Internal Red-Black Tree
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Search in binary tree: Comparing values to go left or right.
                        java
                        Node search(Node root, int key) { if (root.val == key) return root; ... }
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Linear-time sorting (Count sort): Sorting by counting frequencies (not comparisons).
                        java
                        int[] count = new int[maxVal]; for(int x : arr) count[x]++;
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Sorting of linked list: Best done with Merge Sort to avoid random access issues.
                        java
                        // MergeSort logic: split list in half, sort halves, merge back.
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Prefix and suffix trees: Tries used for string pattern matching.
                        java
                        class TrieNode { Map<Character, TrieNode> children; boolean isWord; }
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Describe heap: A complete tree where parent is always
                        ≥is greater than or equal to
                         or
                        ≤is less than or equal to
                         children.
                        java
                        PriorityQueue<Integer> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(Collections.reverseOrder());
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Greedy Algorithm: Making the best local choice at each step (e.g., Dijkstra).
                        java
                        // Example: Always take the largest coin first in change-making.
                        Use code with caution.
                      • NP-complete algorithms: Problems where solutions are fast to check but slow to find (e.g., Traveling Salesman).
                      • Map/reduce & Divide and conquer: D&C splits tasks; Map/Reduce processes them in parallel.
                        java
                        list.parallelStream().map(x -> x*x).reduce(0, Integer::sum);
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Dynamic programming: Storing sub-problem results to avoid re-calculation.
                        java
                        int[] memo = new int[n]; // Fibonacci with memoization
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Fixing cyclic dependency: Use Setter Injection or @Lazy.
                        java
                        @Autowired @Lazy private ServiceB serviceB;
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Static vs Dynamic typing & Duck typing: Java is Static (fixed types); Duck typing is based on methods, not types.
                        java
                        // Duck typing (Pseudo): if(obj.hasMethod("walk")) obj.walk();
                        Use code with caution.
                      3. Collections
                      • General collection interfaces: List, Set, Queue, Map, SortedSet.
                      • Interfaces extending Collection: List, Set, Queue (Map does not).
                      • Difference between ArrayList and LinkedList: ArrayList (Array-based, fast get); LinkedList (Node-based, fast add/remove).
                      • TreeSet vs TreeMap: TreeSet is a Set; TreeMap is a Map. Both are sorted.
                        java
                        SortedSet<String> set = new TreeSet<>();
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Internal structure of HashMap: An array of buckets (Linked Lists/Trees).
                      • Requirements for hashCode: If a.equals(b), then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode().
                      • Resolving collisions: Chaining (LinkedList in bucket) or Open Addressing.
                      • Hashtable vs ConcurrentHashMap: Hashtable locks everything; CHM uses segmented locking.
                        java
                        Map<String, String> map = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Special versions (EnumSet, WeakHashMap):
                        java
                        Map<Key, Value> map = new WeakHashMap<>(); // Keys can be GC'd
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Safe iterators: CopyOnWriteArrayList prevents ConcurrentModificationException.
                        java
                        List<Integer> list = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>();
                        Use code with caution.
                      4. Java Basics
                      • Primitive sizes: int(4 bytes), long(8), char(2), boolean(1-bit).
                      • this vs super: this is current instance; super is parent instance.
                      • Access modifiers: private, default, protected, public.
                      • StringBuffer vs StringBuilder: StringBuffer is thread-safe; StringBuilder is faster.
                      • Immutable classes: State cannot change (e.g., String).
                        java
                        public final class MyImmutable { private final int value; ... }
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Overriding vs Overloading: Overloading (same name, diff params); Overriding (child changes parent method).
                        java
                        @Override public void run() { ... } // Overriding
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Static vs regular blocks: static {} runs once on load; {} runs on every object creation.
                      5. Exceptions
                      • Checked vs Unchecked: Checked (must handle, e.g., IOException); Unchecked (runtime logic, e.g., NullPointerException).
                      • Try/Finally without Catch: Valid for resource cleanup.
                        java
                        try { ... } finally { lock.unlock(); }
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Try-with-resources: Auto-closes AutoCloseable objects.
                        java
                        try (Scanner s = new Scanner(file)) { ... }
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Multi-catch: Catching multiple types in one block.
                        java
                        catch (IOException | SQLException e) { ... }
                        Use code with caution.
                      8. Multithreading
                      • run() vs start(): start() creates a new thread; run() just executes code in the current thread.
                      • wait() vs sleep(): wait() releases lock (Object method); sleep() keeps lock (Thread method).
                      • Volatile keyword: Ensures variable changes are visible across all threads.
                        java
                        private volatile boolean running = true;
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Deadlock example: Thread 1 holds A, waits for B; Thread 2 holds B, waits for A.
                      • Atomic operations: Thread-safe updates without locks.
                        java
                        AtomicInteger count = new AtomicInteger(0); count.incrementAndGet();
                        Use code with caution.
                      13. Spring & 14. Spring Boot
                      • IoC & DI: Framework injects dependencies.
                        java
                        @Autowired public MyController(MyService service) { ... }
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Bean Scopes: singleton (one per app), prototype (new every time).
                      • Actuator: Health/metrics endpoints.
                        properties
                        management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info
                        Use code with caution.
                      • Auto-configuration: @SpringBootApplication guesses config based on classpath jars.
                      16. Hibernate & JPA
                      • SessionFactory vs Session: Factory (one per DB, safe); Session (one per transaction, not safe).
                      • get() vs load(): get returns null; load returns a proxy (lazy).
                      • First vs Second level cache: 1st (Session scope); 2nd (Cluster/Factory scope).
                        java
                        @Cacheable // Enables 2nd level cache for entity
                        Use code with caution.
                      17. SQL
                      • DML vs DDL: SELECT/INSERT (DML) vs CREATE/DROP (DDL).
                      • Joins:
                        sql
                        SELECT * FROM A LEFT JOIN B ON A.id = B.a_id;
                        Use code with caution.
                      • ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.
                      19. JMS & Kafka
                      • Queue vs Topic: Queue (1-to-1); Topic (1-to-Many/Pub-Sub).
                      • Kafka Offset: Position in the partition log.
                      • Idempotent Producer: Prevents duplicate messages on retry.
                        properties
                        enable.idempotence=true
                        Use code with caution.
                      Would you like me to bundle these into a single Markdown file you can download for study?

                          Ask about

                          Turn on your Visual Search History?

                          Google uses its visual recognition technologies to process the images you use to search, like when you search with Google Lens. If you turn on your Visual Search History, Google will save these images from eligible Google services to your Web & App Activity when you’re signed in to your Google Account. You can learn more about this setting and which Google services save images to it at g.co/Search/VisualSearchHistory.

                          How visual search history is used

                          Your Visual Search History may be used to improve your experience on Google services, like letting you revisit your past visual searches. It may be used to develop and improve Google’s visual recognition and search technologies, as well as the Google services that use them.

                          When visual search history is off

                          If you turn this setting off, any previous Visual Search History may still be kept and used to improve Google’s visual recognition and search technologies, unless you delete it from your Web & App Activity.

                          Visual Search History doesn’t affect images saved by other settings, like Gemini Apps Activity.

                          How to manage your Visual Search History

                          You can view, delete, or manage your Visual Search History at activity.google.com. To download your Visual Search History, visit takeout.google.com. Images will be deleted in accordance with your Web & App Activity auto-delete settings, although some types of images may be deleted sooner.

                          Google uses and saves data in accordance with Google Privacy Policy.

                          No thanks
                          Turn on
                          My Ad Centre

                          Search Results

                          Google apps
                          Google Account
                          CHANDRA MOULI SASUBELLI
                          csasubelli@gmail.com
                          Ask about
